title: Integrity and attribution

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Integrity and attribution; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ACM Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct; 2018; section applicable to Integrity and attribution
sources.claim: Academic and professional integrity requires distinguishing allowed collaboration, attributed reference use, licensed reuse, and work represented as one's own under the applicable rules.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Integrity and attribution
sources.claim: Academic and professional integrity requires distinguishing allowed collaboration, attributed reference use, licensed reuse, and work represented as one's own under the applicable rules.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Academic and professional integrity requires distinguishing allowed collaboration, attributed reference use, licensed reuse, and work represented as one's own under the applicable rules.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A learner discusses an algorithm idea with a peer, writes an independent solution, and records the collaboration because the course policy allows conceptual discussion with attribution. Your goal is to choose and justify an ethical action in realistic learning and reuse scenarios. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Debugging and testing loop; Documentation and AI assistance. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Academic and professional integrity requires distinguishing allowed collaboration, attributed reference use, licensed reuse, and work represented as one's own under the applicable rules. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Policies and licenses vary by course and project, common knowledge has limits, and attribution does not automatically grant permission or satisfy an independent-work requirement. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Adding a source link makes any copied solution acceptable, regardless of license or assessment rules.

includes: Distinguish collaboration, reference use, attribution and plagiarism in programming work.
Choose and justify an ethical action in realistic learning and reuse scenarios.
excludes: Policies and licenses vary by course and project, common knowledge has limits, and attribution does not automatically grant permission or satisfy an independent-work requirement.

outcomes: Choose and justify an ethical action in realistic learning and reuse scenarios.

examples.title: Integrity and attribution: direct case
examples.situation: A learner discusses an algorithm idea with a peer, writes an independent solution, and records the collaboration because the course policy allows conceptual discussion with attribution.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A learner discusses an algorithm idea with a peer, writes an independent solution, and records the collaboration because the course policy allows conceptual discussion with attribution.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Check both permission and attribution requirements, describe assistance accurately, and never represent another person's or tool's work as independent evidence of mastery.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Check both permission and attribution requirements, describe assistance accurately, and never represent another person's or tool's work as independent evidence of mastery.

examples.title: Integrity and attribution: boundary case
examples.situation: Code copied from a repository is not submitted when its license and the assignment's independent-work rule do not permit that reuse.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: Code copied from a repository is not submitted when its license and the assignment's independent-work rule do not permit that reuse.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Policies and licenses vary by course and project, common knowledge has limits, and attribution does not automatically grant permission or satisfy an independent-work requirement.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Adding a source link makes any copied solution acceptable, regardless of license or assessment rules.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Choose and justify an ethical action in realistic learning and reuse scenarios.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Adding a source link makes any copied solution acceptable, regardless of license or assessment rules.
misconceptions.correction: Check both permission and attribution requirements, describe assistance accurately, and never represent another person's or tool's work as independent evidence of mastery.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Adding a source link makes any copied solution acceptable, regardless of license or assessment rules.

analogy.example: Sports coaching can model Integrity and attribution: study a play, attempt it, use specific feedback, retry independently, and credit the people or material that helped.
usefulFor: Connecting explanation, feedback, independent transfer, assistance, and integrity
limitations: Learning evidence and reuse permissions are governed by explicit course and software rules, not only informal coaching norms.
Policies and licenses vary by course and project, common knowledge has limits, and attribution does not automatically grant permission or satisfy an independent-work requirement.

Check both permission and attribution requirements, describe assistance accurately, and never represent another person's or tool's work as independent evidence of mastery.

scaffold: Line 2: state the part of the core rule that applies.
Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.

recap.summary: You should now be able to choose and justify an ethical action in realistic learning and reuse scenarios. Keep this boundary in view: Policies and licenses vary by course and project, common knowledge has limits, and attribution does not automatically grant permission or satisfy an independent-work requirement.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Integrity and attribution without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Adding a source link makes any copied solution acceptable, regardless of license or assessment rules.
Apply the model to this case from memory: Code copied from a repository is not submitted when its license and the assignment's independent-work rule do not permit that reuse.
